methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Quantum Computing (found 0 same-stage, 1 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 7 same-sector peers exist in Quantum Computing, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

private.name: planqc
private.rationale: Same sector (Quantum Computing), same stage (Series A)

data.narrative:
Silicon Quantum Computing, led by founder Michelle Simmons, is a Series A-stage quantum computing company that has raised A$83M to date. The quantum computing sector comprises a modest but growing peer universe of 11 companies—3 public (IONQ, RGTI, QBTS) and 8 private firms including IonQ, Quantinuum, Rigetti Computing, D-Wave Quantum, and Infleqtion.

Traditional peer-median valuation proves inapplicable here. The quantum computing peer set lacks sufficient depth at the Series A stage: only 0 same-stage peers, 1 adjacent-stage peer, and 0 similar-tier comparables were identified. With fewer than 3 datapoints in any narrow peer cohort, a same-stage median would be unreliable. Expanding to an all-sector median across the 11 public and private peers would obscure material differences in technology maturity, capital intensity, and commercialization timelines that distinguish early-stage hardware plays from later-stage platforms.

The analysis cannot triangulate a supported valuation range without applying alternative methods (e.g., precedent transaction multiples, DCF, or cost-of-capital analysis) using the available peer set. The primary uncertainty is structural: the sector remains venture-stage with limited exit history and heterogeneous business models, making comparable-company analysis inherently difficult at Series A. Further valuation work requires either deeper transaction data from the private peer cohort or forward-looking financial projections from Silicon Quantum Computing itself.
